volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Andromache (Section 735 - Section 904)

sections.label: Section 735
sections.original: νῦν δ ’ ἐχθρὰ ποιεῖ · τήνδ ’ ἐπεξελθεῖν θέλω
sections.translation: but now is hostile ; against her will I march with my army and bring her into subjection . And when I have arranged that matter as I wish , I will return ; and face to face with my son-in-law I will give my version of the story and hear his .

sections.label: Section 740
sections.original: κἂν μὲν κολάζῃ τήνδε καὶ τὸ λοιπὸν ᾖ
sections.translation: And if he punish her , and for the future she exercise self-control , she shall find me do the like ; but if he storm , I ’ ll storm as well ; and [*] every act of mine shall be a reflex of his own . As for thy babbling , I can bear it easily ;

sections.label: Section 745
sections.original: σκιὰ γὰρ ἀντίστοιχος ὣς φωνὴν ἔχεις ,
sections.translation: for , like to a shadow as thou art , [*] thy voice is all thou hast , and thou art powerless to do aught but talk . Exit Menelaus .

sections.label: Section 747
sections.original: ἡγοῦ , τέκνον μοι , δεῦρ ’ ὑπ ’ ἀγκάλαις σταθείς ,
sections.translation: Lead on , my child , safe beneath my sheltering wing , and thou too , poor lady ; for thou art come into a quiet haven after the rude storm .

sections.label: Section 750
sections.original: ὦ πρέσβυ , θεοί σοι δοῖεν εὖ καὶ τοῖσι σοῖς ,
sections.translation: Heaven reward thee and all thy race , old sire , for having saved my child and me his hapless mother ! Only beware lest they fall upon us twain in some lonely spot upon the road and force me from thee , when they see thy age , my weakness ,

sections.label: Section 755
sections.original: καὶ παῖδα τόνδε νήπιον · σκόπει τάδε ,
sections.translation: and this child ’ s tender years ; take heed to this , that we be not a second time made captive , after escaping now .

sections.label: Section 757
sections.original: οὐ μὴ γυναικῶν δειλὸν εἰσοίσεις λόγον ·
sections.translation: Forbear such words , prompted by a woman ’ s cowardice . Go on thy way ; who will lay a finger on you ? Methinks he will do it to his cost . For by heaven ’ s grace I rule o ’ er many a knight and spearman

sections.label: Section 760
sections.original: πολλῶν θ ’ ὁπλιτῶν ἄρχομεν Φθίαν κάτα ·
sections.translation: bold in my kingdom of Phthia ; yea , and myself can still stand straight , no bent old man as thou dost think ; such a fellow as that a mere look from me will put to flight in spite of my years . For e ’ en an old man , be he brave , is worth a host of raw youths ;

sections.label: Section 765
sections.original: κρείσσων · τί γὰρ δεῖ δειλὸν ὄντ ’ εὐσωματεῖν ;
sections.translation: for what avails a fine figure if a man is a coward ? Exeunt Peleus , Andromache , and Molossus .

sections.label: Section 766
sections.original: μὴ γενοίμαν ἢ πατέρων ἀγαθῶν
sections.translation: Oh ! to have never been born , or sprung from noble sires , the heir to mansions richly stored ;

sections.label: Section 770
sections.original: εἴ τι γὰρ πάσχοι τις ἀμήχανον , ἀλκᾶς
sections.translation: for if aught untoward e ’ er befall , there is no lack of champions for sons of noble parents , and there is honour and glory for them when they are proclaimed scions of illustrious lines ; time detracts not from the legacy these good

sections.label: Section 775
sections.original: ἀνδρῶν ἀφαιρεῖται χρόνος · ἁ δ ’ ἀρετὰ
sections.translation: men leave , but the light of their goodness still burns on when they are dead .

sections.label: Section 778
sections.original: κρεῖσσον δὲ νίκαν μὴ κακόδοξον ἔχειν
sections.translation: Better is it not to win a discreditable victory ,

sections.label: Section 780
sections.original: ἢ ξὺν φθόνῳ σφάλλειν δυνάμει τε δίκαν .
sections.translation: than to make justice miscarry by an invidious exercise of power ; for such a victory , though men think it sweet for the moment , grows barren in time and comes very near being a family reproach .

sections.label: Section 785
sections.original: ταύταν ᾔνεσα ταύταν καὶ φέρομαι βιοτάν ,
sections.translation: This is the life I commend , this the life . I set before me as my ideal , to exercise no authority beyond what is right either in the marriage-chamber or in the state .

sections.label: Section 789
sections.original: ὦ γέρον Αἰακίδα ,
sections.translation: O aged son of Aeacus !

sections.label: Section 790
sections.original: πείθομαι καὶ σὺν Λαπίθαισί σε Κενταύ-
sections.translation: now am I sure that thou wert with the Lapithae , wielding thy famous spear , when they fought the Centaurs ; and on Argo ’ s deck didst pass the cheerless strait beyond the sea-beat Symplegades

sections.label: Section 795
sections.original: κλεινὰν ἐπὶ ναυστολίαν ,
sections.translation: on her voyage of note ; and when in days long gone the son of Zeus spread slaughter round Troy ’ s famous town ,

sections.label: Section 800
sections.original: κοινὰν τὰν εὔκλειαν ἔχοντ ’
sections.translation: thou too didst share his triumphant return to Europe .

sections.label: Section 802
sections.original: ὦ φίλταται γυναῖκες , ὡς κακὸν κακῷ
sections.translation: Alas ! good friends , what a succession of troubles is to-day provided us ! My mistress Hermione within the house ,

sections.label: Section 805
sections.original: πατρός τ ’ ἐρημωθεῖσα συννοίᾳ θ ’ ἅμα ,
sections.translation: deserted by her father and in remorse for her monstrous deed in plotting the death of Andromache and her child , is bent on dying ; for she is afraid her husband will in requital for this expel her with dishonour from his house

sections.label: Section 810
sections.original: ἢ κατθάνῃ κτείνουσα τοὺς οὐ χρὴ κτανεῖν .
sections.translation: or put her to death , because she tried to slay the innocent . And the servants that watch her can scarce restrain her efforts to hang herself , scarce catch the sword and wrest it from her hand . So bitter is her anguish ,

sections.label: Section 815
sections.original: ἔγνωκε πράξασ ’ οὐ καλῶς . ἐγὼ μὲν οὖν
sections.translation: and she hath recognized the villainy of her former deeds . As for me , friends , I am weary of keeping my mistress from the fatal noose ; do ye go in and try to save her life ; for if strangers come , they prove more persuasive than the friends of every day .

sections.label: Section 820
sections.original: καὶ μὴν ἐν οἴκοις προσπόλων ἀκούομεν
sections.translation: Ah yes ! I hear an outcry in the house amongst the servants , confirming the news thou hast brought . Poor sufferer ! she seems about to show a lively grief for her grave crimes ; for she has escaped her servants ’ hands and is rushing from the house , eager to end her life .

sections.label: Section 1.825
sections.original: ἰώ μοί μοι ·
sections.translation: rushing wildly on to the stage . Woe , woe is me ! I will tear my hair and scratch cruel furrows in my cheeks .

sections.label: Section 1.828
sections.original: ὦ παῖ , τί δράσεις ; σῶμα σὸν καταικιῇ ;
sections.translation: My child , what wilt thou do ? Wilt thou disfigure thyself ?

sections.label: Section 1.829
sections.original: αἰαῖ αἰαῖ ·
sections.translation: Ah me ! ah me !

sections.label: Section 1.830
sections.original: ἔρρ ’ αἰθέριον πλοκάμων ἐμῶν ἄπο ,
sections.translation: Begone , thou fine-spun veil ! float from my head away !

sections.label: Section 1.832
sections.original: τέκνον , κάλυπτε στέρνα , σύνδησαι πέπλους .
sections.translation: Daughter , cover up thy bosom , fasten thy robe .

sections.label: Section 2.833
sections.original: τί δέ με δεῖ στέρνα
sections.translation: Why should I cover it ?

sections.label: Section 2.835
sections.original: ἀμφιφανῆ καὶ ἄκρυπτα δε-
sections.translation: My crimes against my lord are manifest and clear , they cannot be hidden .

sections.label: Section 2.836
sections.original: ἀλγεῖς , φόνον ῥάψασα συγγάμῳ σέθεν ;
sections.translation: Art so grieved at having devised thy rival ’ s death ?

sections.label: Section 2.837
sections.original: κατὰ μὲν οὖν στένω
sections.translation: Indeed I am ; I deeply mourn my fatal deeds of daring ; alas ! I am now accursed in all men ’ s eyes !

sections.label: Section 2.840
sections.original: συγγνώσεταί σοι τήνδ ’ ἁμαρτίαν πόσις .
sections.translation: Thy husband will pardon thee this error .

sections.label: Section 841
sections.original: τί μοι ξίφος
sections.translation: Oh ! why didst thou hunt me to snatch away my sword ? Give , oh ! give it back , dear nurse , that I may thrust it through my heart . Why dost thou prevent me hanging myself ?

sections.label: Section 845
sections.original: ἀλλ ’ εἴ σ ’ ἀφείην μὴ φρονοῦσαν , ὡς θάνῃς ;
sections.translation: What ! was I to let thy madness lead thee on to death ?

sections.label: Section 846
sections.original: οἴμοι πότμου .
sections.translation: Ah me , my destiny ! Where can I find some friendly fire ? To what rocky height can I climb above the sea or ’ mid some wooded mountain glen ,

sections.label: Section 850
sections.original: ἵνα θανοῦσα νερτέροισιν μέλω ;
sections.translation: there to die and trouble but the dead ?

sections.label: Section 851
sections.original: τί ταῦτα μοχθεῖς ; συμφοραὶ θεήλατοι
sections.translation: Why vex thyself thus ? on all of us sooner or later heaven ’ s visitation comes .

sections.label: Section 854
sections.original: ἔλιπες ἔλιπες , ὦ πάτερ , ἐπακτίαν
sections.translation: Thou hast left me , O my father ,

sections.label: Section 855
sections.original: ὡσεὶ μονάδ ’ ἔρημον οὖσαν ἐνάλου κώπας .
sections.translation: left me like a stranded bark , all alone , without an oar . My lord will surely slay me ; no home is mine henceforth beneath my husband ’ s roof . What god is there to whose statue I can as a suppliant haste ?

sections.label: Section 860
sections.original: ἢ δούλα δούλας γόνασι προσπέσω ;
sections.translation: or shall I throw myself in slavish wise at slavish knees ? Would I could speed [*] away from Phthia ’ s land on bird ’ s dark pinion , or like that pine-built ship , [*]

sections.label: Section 865
sections.original: πρωτόπλοος πλάτα .
sections.translation: the first that ever sailed betwixt the rocks Cyanean !

sections.label: Section 866
sections.original: ὦ παῖ , τὸ λίαν οὔτ ’ ἐκεῖν ’ ἐπῄνεσα ,
sections.translation: My child , I can as little praise thy previous sinful excesses , committed against the Trojan captive , as thy present exaggerated terror . Thy husband will never listen to

sections.label: Section 870
sections.original: φαύλοις γυναικὸς βαρβάρου πεισθεὶς λόγοις .
sections.translation: a barbarian ’ s weak pleading and reject his marriage with thee for this . For thou wast no captive from Troy whom he wedded , but the daughter of a gallant sire , with a rich dower , from a city too of no mean prosperity . Nor will thy father forsake thee , as thou dreadest ,

sections.label: Section 875
sections.original: προδοὺς ἐάσει δωμάτων τῶνδ ’ ἐκπεσεῖν .
sections.translation: and allow thee to be cast out from this house . Nay , enter now , nor show thyself before the palace , lest the sight [*] of thee there bring reproach upon thee , my daughter . Exit Nurse .

sections.label: Section 879
sections.original: καὶ μὴν ὅδ ’ ἀλλόχρως τις ἔκδημος ξένος
sections.translation: Lo ! a stranger of foreign appearance from some other land

sections.label: Section 880
sections.original: σπουδῇ πρὸς ἡμᾶς βημάτων πορεύεται .
sections.translation: comes hurrying towards us .

sections.label: Section 881
sections.original: ξέναι γυναῖκες , ἦ τάδ ’ ἔστ ’ Ἀχιλλέως
sections.translation: Ladies of this foreign land ! is this the home , the palace of Achilles ’ son ?

sections.label: Section 883
sections.original: ἔγνως · ἀτὰρ δὴ τίς σὺ πυνθάνῃ τάδε ;
sections.translation: Thou hast it ; but who art thou to ask such a question ?

sections.label: Section 884
sections.original: Ἀγαμέμνονός τε καὶ Κλυταιμήστρας τόκος ,
sections.translation: The son of Agamemnon and Clytemnestra ,

sections.label: Section 885
sections.original: ὄνομα δ ’ Ὀρέστης · ἔρχομαι δὲ πρὸς Διὸς
sections.translation: by name Orestes , on my way to the oracle of Zeus at Dodona . But now that I am come to Phthia , I am resolved to inquire about my kinswoman , Hermione of Sparta ; is she alive and well ? for though

sections.label: Section 890
sections.original: ναίουσ ’ ἀφ ’ ἡμῶν πεδί ’ ὅμως ἐστὶν φίλη .
sections.translation: she dwells in a land far from my own , I love her none the less .

sections.label: Section 891
sections.original: ὦ ναυτίλοισι χείματος λιμὴν φανεὶς
sections.translation: Son of Agamemnon , thy appearing is as a haven from the storm to sailors ; by thy knees I pray , have pity on me in my distress , on me of whose fortunes thou art inquiring . About thy knees

sections.label: Section 895
sections.original: σοῖς προστίθημι γόνασιν ὠλένας ἐμάς .
sections.translation: I twine my arms with all the force of sacred fillets .

sections.label: Section 896
sections.original: ἔα ·
sections.translation: Ha ! what is this ? Am I mistaken or do I really see before me the queen of this palace , the daughter of Menelaus ?

sections.label: Section 898
sections.original: ἥνπερ μόνην γε Τυνδαρὶς τίκτει γυνὴ
sections.translation: The same , that only child whom Helen , daughter of Tyndareus , bore my father in his halls ; never doubt that .

sections.label: Section 900
sections.original: ὦ Φοῖβ ’ ἀκέστορ , πημάτων δοίης λύσιν .
sections.translation: O saviour Phoebus , grant us respite from our woe ! But what is the matter ? art thou afflicted by gods or men ?

sections.label: Section 902
sections.original: τὰ μὲν πρὸς ἡμῶν , τὰ δὲ πρὸς ἀνδρὸς ὅς μ ’ ἔχει ,
sections.translation: Partly by myself , partly by the man who wedded me , and partly by some god . On every side I see ruin .

sections.label: Section 904
sections.original: τίς οὖν ἂν εἴη μὴ πεφυκότων γέ πω
sections.translation: Why , what misfortune could happen to a woman as yet childless ,
